## Linear regression is the first serious baseline

In `TEM 0052`, linear regression is not just a statistics recap. It is the first
predictive workflow that students can fully inspect end to end: define features, fit
coefficients, generate predictions, and evaluate the result on held-out data.

That matters because a transparent baseline gives us something defensible to beat. If
a more complex method cannot improve on a well-specified linear model, the extra
complexity is usually hard to justify.

## What OLS is optimizing

Ordinary least squares chooses coefficients that minimize the sum of squared residuals:

$$
\hat{\beta} = \arg\min_{\beta} \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - x_i^\top \beta)^2
$$

This objective makes large prediction errors especially costly, which is why OLS is a
natural starting point for continuous-outcome prediction problems.

For this course, the important point is practical rather than algebraic: OLS gives a
clear rule for turning features into predictions.

## The workflow matters more than the formula

The closed-form solution is useful to know, but students should focus on the modelling
workflow around it.

1. Define the target and the feature set.
2. Split the data before making modelling choices.
3. Fit the baseline linear model.
4. Evaluate in-sample and out-of-sample performance separately.
5. Inspect residuals and coefficient magnitudes before moving to a more flexible model.

::: {.teacher-only}
Ask students what would count as evidence that a linear baseline is already “good
enough” before they reach for ridge, lasso, trees, or boosting.
:::

## Why this concept belongs next to regularization

Linear regression is the reference point for the later regression tools in the course.
Ridge and lasso are not different worlds. They are modifications of the same baseline
workflow when we worry about instability, correlated predictors, or too many weak
features.

That is also why the house-prices lab starts with an ordinary linear model before it
asks students to compare penalized alternatives.

## What to report

When students present a linear regression workflow in this course, they should usually
report:

- the target and feature set,
- the train/test or resampling rule,
- at least one scale-dependent loss such as `MSE`,
- at least one fit measure such as `R^2`,
- and one short interpretation of whether the linear baseline looks stable,
  interpretable, and worth extending.
